one.en: EX TEMPORE REASONS – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking that one of the parties’ children be…

summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – EX TEMPORE REASONS – Where the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) filed an Application in a Proceeding seeking that one of the parties’ children be permitted to attend Court to meet with the presiding judge – Where the ICL gave evidence that the child had conveyed a wish to meet the judicial officer who will be making decisions about the child – Where the purpose of the proposed meeting would not be for the child to express their views – Where there is no relevant power available – Where orders made requesting that the ICL explain to the child the outcome of the Application – Where the ICL is at liberty to read any aspect of the Reasons to the child if the ICL thinks it is appropriate because it will assist the child – Where the ICL is at liberty to provide these Reasons to the child’s psychologist – Application dismissed.

subseq.zh: 标准层(自动摘要)。完整精读可后续升级。
subseq.en: Standard tier (auto-summary). May be upgraded to a full deep-dive later.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

BEHRENS J

These brief ex tempore Reasons deal with an Application in a Proceeding filed by the Independent Children’s Lawyer (“ICL”) in parenting proceedings concerning X, aged nine and Y, aged seven. X and Y have four parents who all love them dearly, but who have not been able to reach agreement about the parenting arrangements between them following the separation of the two mothers.
The Application filed 2 March 2026 seeks that, pursuant to s 100B(2) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”), X be permitted to attend Court for the purpose of meeting with me. I heard the Application in a Proceeding prior to the commencement of the final hearing to determine parenting orders which will occur over the next seven days.
The Application was not opposed by the first respondent mother, was consented to by the applicant mother, and opposed by the fathers on the basis that I do not have the power to do what is asked of me.
The Application is supported by an affidavit of the ICL, Ms Phillips. That affidavit makes clear, and this was confirmed in oral submissions, that it is not sought that I meet with X so that I can have evidence, or better evidence, or the means to obtaining such evidence as to his views, but rather because X has asked to meet the person who will be making such an important decision for him. Ms Phillips gave evidence relevantly that:
(1) X asked her to convey to the Judge that he would like to meet with her;

(2) X has explained that he does not think that he could trust someone to make decisions about him, if he has never met that person;

(3) X’s concern is not that his voice will not be heard. Rather, that he would be comforted by meeting the person who will make decisions about him, in circumstances where his parents cannot agree on what is best for him.

It is perfectly understandable that X would feel this way. He is asking for some comfort and some further information to give him that comfort. I would like to be able to meet him and Y. I would like to be able to give him that comfort. But I am a judge and I exercise judicial power. I can only make orders and take action where I am authorised by the law to do so.
Whatever the case may be in respect of applications for judges to meet with children to obtain further evidence or the means to obtain further evidence about their views, that is not the application before me today. I accept the submission of senior counsel for the fathers that the position in relation to judges meeting children for the purposes of ascertaining their views is at least uncertain, given:
the absence of any mention in the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”) of any relevant power or procedure linked, for example, to s 60CD (How the views of a child are expressed); and
the removal of provisions in the Rules which provided for that possibility and upon which Nicholson CJ relied in ZN & YH & Child Representative [2002] FamCA 453; (2002) FLC 93-101 when his Honour did meet with the children.
Irrespective of what the correct position is with regard to those applications, that is not what I am being asked to do here – rather, frankly, to engage in a kind of therapeutic, information-providing exercise.
The Court is given very wide-ranging powers in what is now pt IX of the Act in child-related proceedings. Nowhere is there a power which could sensibly be interpreted as giving me the power to do what I am asked to do. I reject any submission that s 100B gives me that power – that seems to me to be concerned with leave for children to give evidence (a matter also dealt with in the Rules), and otherwise for their attendance at Court or in the courtroom. I reject the submission that s 102NJ gives me that power. I also reject the submission that s 69 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) gives me that power. Those sections generally deal with the power to make determinations. That is not what I am being asked to do here. The Rules are relevantly silent.
Even if I had been satisfied I had the power, it is unlikely that I would exercise it in the absence of some evidence from a child expert that doing so would be in X’s best interests, particularly noting that X is nine and so I cannot be confident that what he wants to occur is in fact in his best interests. Further, I would be cognisant of the obvious risks of such a meeting limited to the proposed purpose. These include that X may say something which I could not ignore in my decision-making, that X may become distressed, that Y may feel that she has been treated differently (or feel pressured to attend because X wants to), and that either parent may want to put on further evidence about how X had reacted to meeting me, or that I may create expectations in X by meeting with him.
Children have a right under the United Nations Convention on the Rights of the Child to participate in proceedings affecting them. The Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia’s Kids’ Charter provides that “[t]he Courts want you to get all the important information you need, in a way that makes sense to you”.
While I accept that direct participation may be appropriate for some older children, in general the protective policy underlying the law in this country – and particularly for younger children – is that there are other processes available to ensure that these rights are met. Apart from the appointment of ICLs, and the preparation of expert reports, the Court has recently established a “Kids’ Corner” which is designed by experts for children and contains material especially designed so that children can understand more about the process. The Application on behalf of X has made me think that perhaps some consideration might be given in the future to including some clips of judges explaining their roles on that website.
It seems to me that X is a remarkable boy. Even for an adult, it takes a lot of courage to ask for something when you are told (as the ICL gave evidence she told him) that it doesn’t usually happen. For a nine-year-old to find that courage is quite remarkable. It is also remarkable that he does not seem to have told the ICL “I want to speak to the judge so that I can get what I want”, but rather has expressed his needs in much more mature terms and by reference to relationships and trust. All of that augers very well for X’s future.
I am very sorry that I cannot meet with X. I am comforted by the knowledge that he has a very experienced and highly-regarded ICL representing his interests, and that she has provided him with appropriate information, and will be able to answer any further questions he wants to ask. She should be able to read to X any part of these Reasons which she determines it is appropriate he should hear, and otherwise to use her professional judgment to explain my decision to X. I also grant leave for the ICL to provide a copy of these Reasons to X’s therapist in case they are of any assistance to her in her work with X.
